rationale: The citing paper uses Nowlis and Shiv (2005) as prior core evidence that food sampling affects later choice and that experiential sampling involves affective/sensory versus informational components. It cites this work in the problem setup (prior studies focused on sampling one item) and in the background theory that motivates its mechanism discussion. The 2010 paper then positions its own contribution as examining sequential order effects across multiple experiential samples, which is framed as a next step beyond that earlier stream.
relation_description: The citing study builds on Nowlis and Shiv's food-sampling framework by moving from single-sample/distraction effects to sequential sampling order effects in experiential products. It extends the prior line by introducing serial position and memory-recall mechanisms for choices after multiple samples.
